coredata.dc:title: Smart fluorescence aptasensor using nanofiber functionalized with carbon quantum dot for specific detection of pathogenic bacteria in the wound
coredata.dc:creator: Pebdeni, Azam Bagheri
Hosseini, Morteza
Barkhordari, Aref
coredata.dc:description: We developed for the first time a novel and smart nanofiber (NF) network with the electrospinning method to create an amplified fluorescent biosensing platform for the detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) bacteria in wound. The sensing platform is constructed based on surface modification of NFs by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the NF membrane surface, leading to large fluorescence signal amplification. CQDs synthesis was done from orto-phenylenediamine (OPD) with yellow emission fluorescence. Incorporation of CQDs leads to the uniform fluorescence of modified NFs. The proposed biosensing platform can also be applied to detect S. aureus bacteria with high sensitivity and selectivity via a specific aptamer. The linear sensing range for different S. aureus concentration from 10 to 108 CFU/mL and the detection limit of 10 CFU/mL was attained. For the first time these scaffolds were designed for the detection of specific pathogenic bacteria in wound using fluorescence signal of NFs, which can be seen by the naked eye under UV lamp. Enhancing in the fluorescence intensity after putting the modified NFs on skin wounds of mice for 2 h showed the successful application of this novel aptasensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofiber
Carbon quantum dots
S. aureus
Detection
Wound

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Incorporation of CQDs and cross-linking of NFs
Immobilization of the aptamers on the NF-CQDs
S. aureus detection in real sample
Characterization and optical properties of the CQDs
Surface modification of NFs by CQDs and aptamer
Detection of S. aureus by fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM, (B) EDS spectra, (C) Fluorescence intensity with different excitation wavelength, (D) UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs (inset: CQDs solution on light and under UV lamp).

FE-SEM micrograph of (A) PCL NF, (B) PCL-CQDs NF, (C) Cross-linked of PCL-CQDs NF, (D) Modified of NF with aptamer (Inset A, B: Changes in the contact angles of water droplets on the PCL and CQDs NF).

A) FT-IR spectrum of PCL, CQDs-NF, CQDs, and aptamer-NF, B) In vitro cytotoxicity test of the NFs samples against fibroblast L929 cell for 24 h incubation, C) Hemolytic activity of NF, PBS and Triton-X-100 were respectively used as sample, negative control and positive control.
(A) Fluorescence intensity, (B) Calibration curve of modified NF in the presence of different concentration of S. aureus bacteria (101–108 CFU/mL).
Fluorescence microscopic images of PCL NFs (left) and CQD-NFs (right).

Fluorescence microscopic of modified NFs before (A) and after (B) incubation with S. aureus bacteria in different excitation wavelengths (With high magnification, left to right: excited wavelength: 405 nm, 488 nm, and 543 nm).
Macroscopic photographs of (A) the infected wound, (B) covering the NF on the wound; Fluorescence of the NF under UV lamp (C) before and (D, E) after incubation time 1 and 2 h.
Surface functionalize of PCL NF with CQDs and aptamer and the recovery of fluorescence intensity in the presence of S. aureus bacteria.
Comparison of the presented method with other approaches for the S. aureus detection.
Analytical results of different concentration of S. aureus in human serum.

head.ce:title: Smart fluorescence aptasensor using nanofiber functionalized with carbon quantum dot for specific detection of pathogenic bacteria in the wound

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medical Biomaterials Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medical Biomaterials Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Toxicology, Department of Comparative Biosciences, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Toxicology, Department of Comparative Biosciences, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We developed for the first time a novel and smart nanofiber (NF) network with the electrospinning method to create an amplified fluorescent biosensing platform for the detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) bacteria in wound. The sensing platform is constructed based on surface modification of NFs by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the NF membrane surface, leading to large fluorescence signal amplification. CQDs synthesis was done from orto-phenylenediamine (OPD) with yellow emission fluorescence. Incorporation of CQDs leads to the uniform fluorescence of modified NFs. The proposed biosensing platform can also be applied to detect S. aureus bacteria with high sensitivity and selectivity via a specific aptamer. The linear sensing range for different S. aureus concentration from 10 to 108 CFU/mL and the detection limit of 10 CFU/mL was attained. For the first time these scaffolds were designed for the detection of specific pathogenic bacteria in wound using fluorescence signal of NFs, which can be seen by the naked eye under UV lamp. Enhancing in the fluorescence intensity after putting the modified NFs on skin wounds of mice for 2 h showed the successful application of this novel aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most important pathogens for humans is Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) that can cause various life-threatening diseases and food-borne illnesses. Therefore, developing a rapid, simple, sensitive, and specific method for whole-cell detection of S. aureus is highly important [1,2]. In most hospitals, metabolic tests and bacterial cultures are known as standard methods to detect different bacteria. But these processes are quite time consuming, displaying results after several days [3]. Microbial whole-cell detection based on different methods such as microbiology, electrochemistry, colorimetric, fluorescence, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), etc. has gained much attention. Hence, rapid and feasible methods of identifying S. aureus are needed to address these issues [4,5]. Among these methods, fluorometric methods have received particular attention because of the fast response, high sensitivity, and simple operation. Today, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been given more attention as novel luminescence indicators [6]. CQDs have recently appeared as a new type of carbon nanomaterial with less than 10 nm in size. Besides, they have many unique features such as low toxicity, being eco-friendly, having very low density, showing excellent optical properties and wide applications such as drug delivery, catalysis, sensing, bioimaging, and solar cells [7–9]. Electrospun nanofibers (NFs) with diameters ranging from nanometers to micrometers can be produced using the action of an external electric field imposed on a polymer solution or melt [10,11]. NFs have unique characteristics such as flexibility, high surface area to volume ratio, and high porosity with very small pore size. Therefore the non-woven mat NFs have drawn great interest for many biomedical applications involving artificial blood vessels, electrochemical biosensors, drug delivery, antibacterial material, tissue engineering, wound healing, filtration, and SERS substrates [12,13]. Polycaprolactone (PCL) is a synthetic aliphatic polyester with biodegradability, high plasticity, biocompatibility, low melting point, and ductility properties. These features have made it suitable to use in scaffolds production systems [14]. PCL has the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval and exhibits poor cellular response due to its lack of cell affinity [15,16]. Fluorescent NFs are developed with the aptamers as specific bio-receptor, while other research only report incorporation of organic fluorophores and semiconductor quantum dots but they have toxic heavy metals (such as cadmium) [17]. Herein, we incorporated a novel biosensor with benefits of both nanomaterials for the first time: the electrospun NFs with a sizeable surface-area-to-volume ratio for better surface modification, and the CQDs intercalated on the NFs to generate a strong fluorescence signal which is appropriate to design and construct a smart NF that shows fluorescence sensing in the presence of pathogenic bacteria in the wound with high sensitivity, rapid and quantitative detection of S. aureus (Scheme 1 ). The polymer, CQDs, and aptamer do not have any cytotoxicity and are safe for use on wounds, thus these smart biosensors have good biocompatibility [18–21]. Few studies are available in the literature where NFs are used for biosensing through the incorporation of aptamers, whereas most research is on drug delivery application of NFs for wound healing, and thus this research is completely novel and shows the excellent application of NFs for biosensing of bacteria in wound [22–25]. However, the application of modified NFs has not yet been reported for the whole-cell detection of microbial pathogens in the literature. The electrospun NF membranes with appropriate fiber diameters were synthesized from PCL polymer solution and the surface functionalization by CQDs from OPD was done with yellow emission and good quantum yield. Optimized immobilization of the specific aptamer was carried out to improve S. aureus detection in order to achieve enhanced fluorescence response and sensitivity. The large area of the material's surface led to more biological events and facilitation of the kinetics of the assay. The electrospun biosensor was tested with other bacteria to confirm the bio-modified nanofibrous membranes' specificity and biosensing performance for S. aureus bacteria.

PCL polymer, chloroform, methanol, glutaraldehyde, Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were purchased from Merck. The 45 mer specific aptamer for S. aureus (5′-GCG CCC TCT CAC GTG GCA CTC AGA GTG CCG GAA GTT CTG CGT TAT-3′) was purchased from Pishgam Biotech Company (Tehran, Iran). All the other employed chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and were used without any further purification.

NFs were prepared with an electrospinning device of Fanavaran Nano-meghyas Co. (model ES100). The analysis of the surface and morphologies of the synthesized NFs was done using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Supra 400VP, Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). To determine the average diameter and standard deviation of the fiber, Image J software was used for at least 50 NFs. The morphology and disperse of the CQDs were further characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM Zeiss, EM10C, 80 KV, Germany). A fluorescence spectrophotometer conducted photoluminescence emission measurements (RF-5301 PC, Shimadzu, and Kyoto, Japan). A solid holder of photoluminescence was used to measure the fluorescence intensity of the NFs. The analysis of the functional groups was carried out using FT-IR spectrophotometers (Shimadzu-8400S spectrometer). The fluorescence images were acquired using a fluorescence microscope (Olympus IX81). The oven used for the synthesis of CQDs was provided. Absorbance spectra were attained using a UV–Vis spectrometer (PerkinElmer lambda 25) spectrometer in the range of 200–800 nm. All analysis was done at room temperature under an ambient condition.

0.5 g OPD was dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water, the mixed solution was put in a 50 mL steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C temperature for 5 h, and then the products were cooled at room temperature. In this way, the original CQDs aqueous solution was obtained. After that, the solution was purified by a 0.22 μm filter membrane and centrifuged. This solution was stored at 4 °C for future applications.

To prepare the polymer solution for the electrospinning process, the PCL polymer was dissolved in chloroform and methanol as solvents with a ratio of 3:1 (v/v %) with 9% and 12% (wt. %). PCL was dissolved in solvents on a constant magnetic stirrer for 3 h. After complete dissolution of PCL, the resulting dispersion was transferred to a 5 mL syringe fitted with a metallic needle with an inner diameter of 23-gauge. The needle was in turn connected to a high voltage power supply and put in the syringe pump. The electrospinning process was conducted under sorted parameters; applied voltage = 15 kV; flow rate = 0.5 mL/h; the distance between needle-collector = 13 cm. PCL NFs were collected on a flat aluminum foil. All processes were conducted under ambient conditions such as 25 ± 3 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of CQDs and cross-linking of NFs
ce:section.ce:para: 1 × 1 cm pieces of NFs were cut and immersed in the CQD solution while shaken on the stirrer for 24 h in a dark place. Then, the NFs are taken out of the CQD solution and prepared for cross-linking. Thus, prepared electrospun NFs were placed on top of the 25% aqueous glutaraldehyde in a desiccator for 24 h at room temperature to become cross-linked; therefore, NF hydrophilic properties will be improved. After 24 h, the NFs were taken out of the desiccator and dried at room temperature for 24 h in order to remove the unreacted glutaraldehyde [26]. Before immobilization of the aptamers on the NF-CQDs, they were treated with glutaraldehyde.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of the aptamers on the NF-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The immobilization of the aptamers for microbial whole-cell biosensing required surface modification of the NFs by specific aptamers. The NFs were immersed in 5 μL of 45 mer aptamer (100 μM) and 50 μL binding buffer (PH 7.4) (50 mM Tris-HCl, 10 mM MgCl2, 100 mM NaCl, 5 mM KCl) and shaken in a dark place at 37 °C for 120 min. Then, the NFs were taken out and washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The resulted fluorescence emission of the modified NFs was studied before and after the immobilization.

Determination of the material cytotoxicity was done via the MTT assay. The NF membranes, 5 × 5 mm, were sterilized by ethanol 70%. RPMI fresh media without NF samples were designated as control. The samples were used to treat the fibroblast cells L929 (NCBI C161), 104 cells/mL, grown for 24 h. After another 24 h using an MTT assay, optical density was quantified. Cell viability was quantified as the optical density of the treated wells relative to the non-treated wells (0%). Performing MTT assay is necessary to measure cellular activity, and 10% V/V MTT solution (5 mg/mL) in the culture medium was added to the cell. The samples were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C, and formazan crystals transformed from MTT by mitochondrial reductase were dissolved by isopropanol. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured using a microplate reader (Bio Tek ELx 808, USA).

Hemolysis process involves the denaturation of cells through the interaction between NF and the cell surface. For hemolysis testing, blood samples were taken from three healthy participants. 3 mL of blood was taken in sterile tubes containing EDTA (anticoagulant). To separate the red blood cells (RBCs), the obtained samples were centrifuged at 1500 RPM for 10 min. Then, the RBCs were washed in PBS five times. The pellet of cleaned RBCs was suspended in PBS and diluted to 50 mL. 1 mL of diluted PBS was transferred to vials, and 1 L of Triton X was added for RBC lysis, which served as a positive control, while RBCs in saline served as a negative control. The absorbance (OD) at 540 nm was determined. The percentage of hemolysis was calculated by the equation:
Hemolysis ratio = (OD (sample) – OD (negative control))/(OD (positive control) − OD (negative control)) × 100%

The bacteria used in this study were Escherichia coli (ATCC35218), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC 10145), Salmonella typhymorium (ATCC 14028), S. aureus (ATCC 29213), and Bacillus subtilis (ATCC 168). Cultures were grown overnight in nutrient broth under moderate shaking. After reactivation, the cells were cultured in nutrient agar plates. Then some of their colonies were re-suspended in the buffer and their optical density was measured at 600 nm (OD 600). The number of cells was estimated using the plate counting method as a standard method and the cells were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The plate counting method as a gold standard method was used to estimate the number of bacterial cells. 0.5 mL of the bacteria were placed on NF samples (1 × 1 cm) and incubated under shaking at room temperature.

To evaluate the specificity and selectivity of the modified NFs, four kinds of bacteria including E. coli, S. typhimorium, Bacillus subtilis and P. aeruginosa with the same concentration of bacteria were tested. The fluorescence intensity of the NFs after incubation with individual different bacteria was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S. aureus detection in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensing performance of the S. aureus aptamer-NF biosensor, the real samples were prepared. The performance of the proposed sensor was assessed through spiking three known concentrations of S. aureus (107, 105, and 103 CFU/mL) bacteria in ten times diluted human serum. The fluorescence intensity was measured as described previously. At the end, the percentage of recovery of this aptasensor was calculated. In order to show the capacity of the smart NFs for the detection of specific bacteria in other samples, we also tested milk as a food sample and calculated the recovery percentage in it. The milk was purchased from local supermarkets and diluted 20 times with deionized water.

Animal infection experimentations were accomplished with an adult mouse (180 gr weight) that was accommodated under optimum conditions of temperature and light, and also fed a standard diet, water, and libitum. A 5 × 5 cm2 area of hair on the back of the mice was shaved using hair remover under anesthesia using ketamine and xylazine. Then, the removal of 20 × 20 mm square of skin was done. Afterward, bacterial infection at the wound site was performed by placing 10 μL droplets containing 108 CFU/mL concentration of S. aureus from an overnight bacterial culture in the stationary phase. The wound was photographed for three days to observe local inflammatory reactions visually. The NF membranes were placed on the wound surface for detection of S. aureus and covered using modified NFs for 2 h. The fluorescence intensity of the NF samples was monitored before and after the incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: We have successfully prepared CQDs with strong yellow fluorescence through a hydrothermal assisted facile method using OPD. We synthesized CQDs from OPD as the source with water via the hydrothermal method. TEM images of prepared CQDs are illustrated in Fig. 1 A which display the uniform dispersion and the size of these nanoparticles. The size of the synthesized CQDs was around 5–6 nm. Energy-dispersive X-ray elemental mapping showed overall uniform distribution of C, N, and O in the surface of the NF (Fig. 1B). To assess the proper composition of the CQDs, the energy-dispersive analysis of X-rays (EDX) was utilized. The presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen with an abundance of 49.89, 40.33, and 9.78%, respectively, was proven by the EDX spectra. The optical absorption and fluorescence properties of CQDs were characterized using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence of CQDs in various excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 to 440 nm wavelength is shown in Fig. 1C. Excitation of CQDs at 420 nm led to the strongest emission at around 600 nm. The UV–Vis spectrum displayed strong optical absorption in the region of 200–800 nm. The absorption peaks at 270 and 410 nm are due to the π-π* transition and n-π* transition, respectively (Fig. 1D). The observed short- and long-wavelength absorption bands here were generally attributed to the π→π* transition of the sp2 CC and n→π* transition of CO of CQDs, respectively, in reference to the literature [18,27,28]. The CQDs color was light yellow but under UV illumination at 369 nm excitation a strong yellow fluorescence emission was seen (Fig. 1D inset).

The electrospinning technique is an excellent candidate to fabricate functional nanomaterials for sensing because it is a cost-effective process to synthesize NF scaffolds with uniform and nanoscale fibers, without needing coagulation chemistry or high temperatures and can be easily controlled for NF fabrication using biocompatible materials [28–30]. The hydrophobic properties of PCL make it insoluble in aqueous solvents. Typical excellent solvents were used to dissolve PCL including chloroform, methylene chloride, hexafluoro propanol, and mixtures of them. We used a 70:30 (v/v) ratio of chloroform and methanol as the suitable solvent for PCL [31,32]. Two concentrations of PCL polymeric solution (9 and 12 wt %) were prepared in chloroform and methanol as solvents. These solutions were electrospun under the same condition. The FE-SEM images of two concentrations of PCL NFs are shown in Fig. S1. c

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification of NFs by CQDs and aptamer
ce:section.ce:para: FE-SEM images of PCL and PCL-CQD NFs are illustrated in Fig. 2 (A, B). The FE-SEM images corroborated that surface functionalization by CQDs and cross-linking by glutaraldehyde and aptamers did not alter the morphology of the NF networks. By placing the NFs into a desiccator filled with saturated glutaraldehyde vapor, the PCL-CQD NFs could be reasonably cross-linked. FE-SEM images of NFs cross-linked by glutaraldehyde are shown in Fig. 2C. After surface modification of NFs with CQDs, they were cross-linked by the vapor of glutaraldehyde. The network and morphology of the NFs were retained after cross-linking. This reaction caused the activation of the carboxylic group by glutaraldehyde and the generation of free amine groups on the surface of the CQDs with an OPD source and the production of CN bonds between them. Finally, the NF network grew and the biodegradability of the NFs decreased and other properties such as solubility and stability increased. An increase in these properties eases their use for different bio-applications specially biosensing. There is no difference in the fluorescence intensity of PCL-CQD NFs before and after crosslinking. After electrospinning, the average diameter of the PCL NFs was 700 nm while the diameter of the NFs after surface modification by CQDs and cross-linking by glutaraldehyde increased and led to an average diameter of around 1 μm. This showed surface modification and cross-linking do not have a degradation effect on the NF structure and their suitable morphology was maintained. Contact angle measurements were also performed to evaluate the surface characteristics of these PCL NFs. One of the essential factors that influence the attachment of aptamer and microbial cells to surfaces is membrane hydrophilicity. We examined this by estimating the water contact angles for PCL NF membranes before and after modification by CQDs. Thus, NFs are a dominant factor that governs the initial adhesion of the aptamer and bacterial cells to the NFs [33]. Contact angle measurements before and after treatment of PCL NFs were performed to determine the effect of the surface modifications on the surface hydrophilicity of the NFs. Fig. 2 A, B (inset) shows the difference between the contact angles of the PCL NFs and CQD-PCL NFs with water droplets were 134.72° and 33.47°, respectively. The presence of incorporated CQDs played an essential role in imparting the scaffold with these novel characteristics. Fig. S2 shows the EDX analysis of PCL-CQD NFs that showed the presence of CQDs in the NFs, because PCL NFs do not have any amine groups but after modification with CQDs, nitrogen groups can be seen. Also, elemental mapping of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and overlay image of all elements validates the suitable surface modification of the NFs by CQDs. Fluorescent investigations additionally demonstrated the absorption and trapping of the fluorescent properties imparted by the CQDs to PCL scaffolds with fluorescence in a similar excitation range as seen for CQDs. UV–Vis spectroscopy of CQDs showed two peaks in the 270 and 410 nm emission range. These results were used as a reference to set the excitation/emission wavelengths for the NFs modified with CQDs. Also, the excitation of the PCL-CQD NFs in 400 nm resulted in a clear emission signal with high fluorescence intensity at the same emission of CQDs. As it is illustrated in the fluorescence spectra in Fig. S3A, the emission peak of the electrospun NFs is similar to pure CQDs solution suggesting good dispersion of CQDs on the surface of the NF structures as a result of surface modification and crosslinking. There are some strategies for surface modification of NFs without causing a decrease in the bioactivity of ligands or properties of NFs [34]. Fig. S3B showed of fluorescence intensity of NFs after 60 days in 4 °C, displaying the very good stability of emission of this nanostructure after this time. In this process, the fluorescence of the modified NFs was retained even after cross-linking and washing. It showed the optical properties of the CQDs were remained after transforming into the surface of the NF structures. The fluorescence intensity of the PCL-CQD NFs verified that this outstanding characteristic of CQDs found in NF composite structures makes them novel nanostructures for bio-applications. The surface area for attaching the aptamers to NFs was significantly increased by surface modification with CQDs, which enhanced the binding effect and reaction rate. The COOH groups on the surface of the NF-CQDs were attached to amino groups on the nucleotide structure by π-π stacking interactions. Immobilization of biological ligands such as aptamers is ideal for giving sensing properties to the NFs. We chose aptamers as a good bio-receptor for modifying the NFs because aptamers have excellent properties [35]. As shown in Fig. 2D, the immobilization of aptamers on the surface of the NFs does not lead to any changing in the morphology of NFs and we cannot see the aptamer on the surface of NFs because they have a very small size which is not visible in FE-SEM. The quenching effect caused by the presence of aptamers in the fluorescence intensity of CQD-NFs is shown in Fig. S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S. aureus by fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of PCL-CQD NFs interacting with bacteria is a result of the electrospun structures after bio-modification with the aptamers. The aptamers on the modified NF interacted with S. aureus, thus the intensity of fluorescence emission of the modified NFs was returned. The untreated NFs do not show any affinity for binding to the surface of bacteria and the fluorescence intensity returned after incubation. To improve sensitivity, the sensing conditions were optimized. The surface modification of the electrospun membranes by aptamers was optimized at different times as shown in Fig. S5A. No difference between the fluorescence intensity of the aptamer-NFs in the primary 30 min was seen, but after spending more time, at 120 min, the fluorescence intensity of aptamer-NFs was quenched, that validated the efficient aptamers immobilization on the surface of the NFs. Due to high surface area and suitable modifications by aptamers and yellow emission of CQDs, the NF mats were significantly improved for detection and pathogen binding events. Fig. S5B illustrates the time response of the sensor conductance signal for the same concentration of the S. aureus sample. During the initial time around 30 min, the fluorescence intensity of the NFs cannot return very well because the proteins in the membrane of the target bacteria needed more time to interact with the aptamer and separate from the NFs. The fluorescence intensity signal of NFs increased and returned when the incubation time of the specific bacteria was 60 min. After 120 min, the enhancing in the fluorescence intensity was the most. This time is rapid and suitable for the separation of the aptamers from the surface of the NFs. Experiments using various concentrations of S. aureus bacteria was done to verify the sensitivity and performance of the electrospun biosensor. The pathogen detection outcomes are displayed in Fig. 4 . The conventional biosensor displayed linear sensing reaction for different S. aureus concentrations from 10 to 108 CFU/mL, with a linear regression equation of y = 78.963x + 111.68, R2 = 0.9657. Its sensitivity (detection limit) was 10 CFU/mL. The specific binding caused a structural rearrangement of the aptamer after binding to specific bacteria, eventually leading to separation from the NFs and the return of the fluorescence of the NFs. These emissions can be seen under the UV lamp, which is a sign of S. aureus detection. Higher sensitivity and broader linear response for the detection of pathogens was seen in the functionalized electrospun aptasensor. The unique porous nanostructures and high surface area make the electrospun mat a suitable biosensor in the solid state, providing a faster mass transfer rate which enhanced the aptasensing reaction rate. The results display that after 1 h high sensitivity of modified NF occurs. In addition, this aptamer-NF biosensor exhibited long-term stability and specificity toward S. aureus bacteria in the presence of protein-membranes in the surface of the whole cell of S. aureus bacteria.

Fig. 5 is related to fluorescence microscopic images of PCL and PCL-CQDs NFs. PCL NFs do not show any fluorescence emission. Fig. 5 (right) represents the fluorescence image of the CQDs- NF sample (magnification 200 μm). NFs with yellow fluorescence emission were observed due to the optical property of CQDs. It should be noted that a uniform fluorescence emission was observed throughout all electrospun NFs confirming the uniform distribution of CQDs in the surface of NFs without aggregation. The enhancement in the fluorescence emission was observed for aptamer-NFs in contact with the whole-cell of S. aureus bacteria which can be explained by the attachment of the aptamer to the cell membrane of bacteria. The fluorescence intensity shows a maximum intensity and returns after 2 h incubation with S. aureus with a higher intensity as the incubation time with specific bacteria increases. Electrostatic interactions played a role in microbial adhesion to the aptamers on the surface of pathogenic bacterial cells that have a negative charge, interacting with the positive charge from nitrogen in the bases of oligonucleotide aptamer.

The good binding capability of the NFs by bio-receptors improved the performance in the selection of the specific targets. The selectivity and specific binding of aptamers for capturing the S. aureus bacteria were evaluated and results are presented in Fig. S7. Other bacterial cells cannot interact with the aptamer at the same concentration similar to S. aureus, and thus do not display similar fluorescence intensity with S. aureus. This platform enables highly selective detection of whole-cell S. aureus. The recovery of the emission intensity occurred only in the presence of S. aureus.

The current signal considerably changed when the sensor was exposed to the bacteria in the real samples, and this is because of the high sensitivity and specificity of the aptasensor. The results are shown in Fig. S8 which displays that this NF biosensor is able to identify the whole-cell of S. aureus in real samples and can be used for further applications of bacterial infection detections in wound. There was a notable change in fluorescence intensity of the NFs following the introduction of mixtures containing S. aureus relative to that experienced without a target. This revealed that this biosensor was highly specific for S. aureus bacteria. As noted before, this aptamer-based NF platform could detect bacteria from the real sample efficiently with 92%–95% recovery at different concentrations (107, 105, 103 CFU/mL) of S. aureus (Table 2 ). In order to assess the capture sensitivity of the platform, human serum samples were spiked with different low bacterial concentrations of S. aureus. After that, an appropriate amount of sample was added to the NFs. Also, the ability of the smart NFs for the detection of target bacteria in food samples such as milk was tested and the result showed a good recovery of about 80–91% (Table S1). Thus these results demonstrate the generality of the feasibility of the capture platform for bacterial enrichment in clinical and food samples. The excellent performance of the capture platform belonged to the high selectivity and affinity of the aptamers. Experimental data recommended that the designed capture platform is sensitive enough to detect low concentrations of bacteria in the human serum of patients.

Fig. 7 shows the in-vivo experimental design. When the skin wound was infected, the prepared NFs of the same size as the wound, meaning 20 × 20 mm, were put on the surface of the wound. The fluorescence emission of the NFs was seen immediately under UV lamp as control and after 1 h and 2 h as optimum incubation time, the fluorescence of the NF was monitored. The enhancement in the emission of the NFs on the skin wound occurred. After taking out the smart NFs from the skin wound of the mouse, they were washed with PBS several times to remove the blood from the aptasensor and the fluorescence intensity of it was investigated at 400 nm exciting wavelength. Enhancement in the fluorescence emission can be seen in Fig. S9 and inset is related to the NFs under UV lamp. A slight shift in the emission wavelength was seen which probably is related to the attachment or penetration of the blood and other agents from the wound in the pore of the NFs.

An electrospun biosensor based on aptamers and the fluorescence of CQDs was developed and optimized for the simple, cheap, portable and sensitive identification of S. aureus bacteria in wounds. We explored crosslinking as an effective method to incorporate fluorescent CQDs in polymeric NFs as a biosensor. Fluorescent PCL-CQD NFs were successfully synthesized by a simple electrospinning process. It was found that these composite NFs incorporate the luminescent CQDs through their interactions with polymer chains. We presented a selective, reproducible, and stable NF aptasensor for the direct detection of bacteria in infected wounds. Resulting from the outstanding features of the nanostructure and biocompatibility of the electrospun NFs as a solid and portable biosensor, the proposed sensing platform displayed linear detection response for S. aureus samples. After S. aureus was captured by the aptamers conjugated to the modified NFs, the sensitivity was observed in various concentrations of bacteria leading to the enhancement in fluorescence emission under UV lamp which can be seen with the naked eye. Through changing the specific aptamer with appropriate bio-receptors, we can extend the functionalization process to other microbial or viral organisms, which enables the detection of targets in the different substrates such as burn wound or poisoned food. Other biosensor detection mechanisms can be developed through the implementation of the electrospinning and functionalization method proposed in this study.

Azam Bagheri pebdeni designed and performed the experiments and also wrote the manuscript. Morteza Hosseini as supervisor and the corresponding author designed experiments, sponsored the study and made all the arrangements. Aref Barkhordari performed the animal infection study. All the authors reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.123454.
